description: Fresh basil from your kitchen windowsill — tastes nothing like the supermarket kind. Grows fast, smells amazing, harvest weekly for pesto or caprese. Seed to harvest in 3-4 weeks.

care.water_frequency: Every 3-5 days, keep soil moist but never soggy
care.light_preferred: Full sun 6+ hours daily — south-facing window
care.light_also_ok: Bright indirect (survives but leggy, less flavorful. Consider a grow light.)
care.humidity_action: Good air circulation is more important than humidity. Stagnant wet air causes fungal issues.
care.soil_types: Rich, well-draining potting mix with compost
care.repot_frequency: Annual plant — start new from seed every 3-4 months for year-round harvest
care.difficulty: Easy — fast and rewarding, but needs consistent sun and water. Bolts if stressed.
care.watering_guide: Keep soil consistently moist like a wrung-out sponge. Wilts dramatically when thirsty — water immediately and it bounces back. Water the soil, not the leaves.
care.light_guide: South-facing window is non-negotiable for flavorful basil. If you only have north-facing light, add a grow light. More sun = more essential oils = better taste.
care.tips: The #1 rule: pinch off flower buds the moment you see them. Flowering = end of leaf production. Harvest from the top, cutting just above a leaf pair — this triggers two new branches. More you harvest, bushier it grows.
care.toxicity_note: Completely safe. All parts are edible — leaves, flowers, and even seeds.
common_problems: Bolting / flowering (too hot, too stressed — pinch flowers immediately)
Wilting then yellow leaves (underwatering — basil is dramatic but recovers fast)
Black spots on leaves (fungal — water the soil not the leaves, improve air circulation)
common_pests: Aphids (tiny green bugs clustering on new growth — blast with water or insecticidal soap)
Slugs (if growing outdoors — use copper tape or beer traps)
Whiteflies (tiny white moths — yellow sticky traps work well)

description: A woody Mediterranean herb that thrives on neglect. Prefers poor soil, dislikes too much water, and rewards you with fragrant evergreen needles year-round. One plant provides all the rosemary you will ever need.

care.water_frequency: Every 7-10 days when top inch of soil is dry
care.water_winter: Every 10-14 days, err on the dry side
care.light_also_ok: Bright indirect (survives a few weeks but gets leggy and loses flavor)
care.humidity_action: Good air circulation is critical indoors. Open a window nearby when possible. Do not mist.
care.soil_types: Well-draining, sandy, slightly alkaline soil. Mix in extra sand or perlite.
care.difficulty: Medium — easy outdoors, trickier indoors. The #1 indoor killer is overwatering + poor air circulation.
care.watering_guide: Let the top inch dry out before watering. Rosemary prefers being slightly dry to slightly wet. If leaves start going crispy, you waited too long. If stems go soft, you watered too much.
care.light_guide: Absolutely needs direct sunlight. A south-facing windowsill is ideal. If stems get leggy and needles are sparse, it is telling you it needs more light.
care.tips: Harvest by cutting sprigs (not individual leaves) above a leaf node to encourage branching. Woody stems at the base are normal. Flowers are edible and attract pollinators. Prune after flowering to stay bushy.
care.toxicity_note: Completely safe. Leaves and flowers are both edible.
common_problems: Crispy brown needle tips (too dry indoors or underwatering — check if roots are healthy first)
Powdery mildew (white dusty coating — improve air circulation, reduce crowding)
Root rot (overwatering in heavy soil — switch to terracotta pot with sandy mix)
common_pests: Spider mites (especially in dry indoor air — increase ventilation, spray with water)
Whiteflies (yellow sticky traps near the plant)
Powdery mildew (fungal, not a pest — improve air circulation)
